serving.natural_language_portion: One paper thosai typically weighs approximately 80–120 g; a standard serving of 100 g is used for nutrient calculations.

nutrient_source.title: Thosai, Paper — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database lists Paper Thosai (F05010007) with direct per-100g nutrient values. All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — dosa / fermented rice-lentil pancake
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for paper thosai found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous food — plain dosa (fermented rice and urad dal pancake) — as reported in peer-reviewed GI tables (Atkinson et al., 2021, AJCN). GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range (≤55); gi_category is therefore 'low' per the standard classification (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70). Paper thosai uses the same batter composition but is spread thinner and cooked crispier, which may marginally increase GI due to greater starch gelatinisation and surface area; GI 55 is therefore a conservative analogous estimate. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 45.8) / 100 = 25.2 per 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 437 mg/100 g with low potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 437 mg/100 g is borderline elevated for hypertension management; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced salt in batter preparation, and account for additional sodium from sambar and chutney accompaniments, as total meal sodium can easily exceed 600 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate load (45.8 g/100 g), GI 55 (low-medium boundary), high glycemic load (25.2), and dietary fibre below 3 g/100 g threshold
type2_diabetes.guidance: Paper thosai delivers a high glycemic load of 25.2 per 100 g serving due to its refined rice-dominant batter, GI of 55 at the low-medium boundary, and insufficient dietary fibre (2.7 g/100 g); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion to half a thosai, pair with high-fibre sambar and vegetable chutneys, and monitor postprandial glucose response, as the thin crispy preparation accelerates starch digestion compared to thicker dosa variants.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat (10.8 g/100 g) with borderline sodium (437 mg/100 g) and low dietary fibre (2.7 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Paper thosai contains 10.8 g fat/100 g (estimated saturated fat ~1.8 g) and 437 mg sodium/100 g, which are borderline concerns for cardiovascular disease; individuals should limit portion size, choose oil-minimised preparation, and pair with fibre-rich accompaniments to partially offset the low dietary fibre content (2.7 g/100 g, below the 3 g/100 g cardioprotective threshold).

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined rice-dominant batter with insufficient fibre and borderline sodium; no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Paper thosai lacks anti-inflammatory nutrients (no omega-3, polyphenols, or meaningful antioxidants) and its borderline sodium (437 mg/100 g) may worsen RA-related tissue swelling; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consume sparingly and pair with turmeric-containing sambar to partially offset the pro-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paper thosai (also spelled paper dosai or paper dosa) is a very thin, large, and crispy fermented rice-and-lentil pancake popular in Singapore's mamak and South Indian hawker stalls. It is made from a batter of fermented rice flour and urad dal (black lentil), spread extremely thinly on a hot griddle and cooked with a small amount of oil until golden and crispy. The result is a large, paper-thin crepe with a satisfying crunch, distinct fermented tang, and mild savoury flavour. It is typically served rolled or folded and accompanied by sambar (a lentil-vegetable soup) and coconut or tomato chutney. Per 100 g, paper thosai provides 305 kcal, 6.4 g protein, 10.8 g fat, 45.8 g carbohydrate, 2.7 g dietary fibre, and 437 mg sodium. Its glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low-medium boundary, classified low per standard GI scale) with a high glycemic load of 25.2 per 100 g, making it a significant glycemic concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes despite the technically low GI classification. The sodium content is borderline elevated, requiring caution for hypertension management. It is naturally vegetarian, vegan, and gluten-free. In Singapore, paper thosai is a common breakfast and supper item, widely available at mamak stalls and Indian hawker centres.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05010007), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021, AJCN) using the closest approved GI source equivalent (plain dosa). All mandatory multilingual names are present. Confidence is high for nutrient data; GI carries medium confidence due to analogous estimation.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Paper Thosai (F05010007) as of 2026. Saturated fat estimated at 1.8 g/100 g based on typical oil composition used in paper thosai preparation (palm oil or vegetable oil), cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central dosa entries; this is an approximation and should be verified against a direct HPB or ICMR-NIN measurement. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from plain dosa per Atkinson et al. (2021, AJCN); no direct GI measurement for paper thosai was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI 55 is classified as 'low' per the standard GI scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70); however, the glycemic load of 25.2 per 100 g serving is high due to the large carbohydrate content (45.8 g/100 g), making this food a significant glycemic concern in practice despite the low GI category. Glycemic load calculated as (55 × 45.8) / 100 = 25.2. All core macros (protein_g 6.4, fat_g 10.8, carbohydrate_g 45.8) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification required.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input, populated all mandatory sections, added multilingual names, health context, ontology, and LLM description.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'close' to 'closest_parent'. Removed duplicate alias entries.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index value of 55. Per standard GI classification (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), GI 55 is classified as low. Updated gi_source notes and health_context type2_diabetes key_factor and notes to reflect the corrected classification while preserving the clinical warning about high glycemic load.
